title: Word ladder
description: Find shortest transformation sequence using BFS

approaches.name: Brute Force Backtracking
approaches.summary: Explore all possible sequences from the start word to the end word by recursively trying every word in the word list that differs by one character, without pruning. This is impractical for typical inputs due to factorial growth but serves as a baseline for understanding the problem.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Backtracking
ladders.desc: Complete implementation exploring all possible transformation sequences from start to end word without pruning.
ladders.explanation:
This brute force backtracking approach systematically explores every possible path from the start word to the end word by recursively trying all words in the word list that differ by exactly one character. The intuition is that by exploring all possibilities without any optimization, we guarantee finding the shortest transformation sequence if one exists, though at an enormous computational cost. The algorithm begins with the start word and recursively explores all valid next words (those differing by one character and not yet visited in the current path). It maintains the current path and tracks the shortest path found. When the end word is reached, it compares the current path length to the best found so far. The time complexity is O(n * m!) where n is the word length and m is the number of words, due to exploring all permutations of words. Space complexity is O(m) for recursion depth and path storage. This connects to the parent problem by providing a baseline solution that demonstrates why more efficient approaches (like BFS) are necessary. Common mistakes include not checking for one-character differences correctly, forgetting to backtrack properly, and not handling cycles (which this approach avoids by tracking visited words in the current path). The real code pattern involves a recursive function that explores neighbors and backtracks.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize shortest_path as empty list
2. Define recursive function backtrack(current_word, current_path, visited_in_path)
   a. Add current_word to current_path and visited_in_path
   b. If current_word equals end_word:
      i. If current_path is shorter than shortest_path or shortest_path is empty, update shortest_path
   c. Else:
      i. For each word in word_list:
         - If word differs from current_word by exactly one character AND word not in visited_in_path:
            * Recursively call backtrack(word, current_path, visited_in_path)
   d. Remove current_word from current_path and visited_in_path (backtrack)
3. Call backtrack(start_word, [], set())
4. Return shortest_path or 0 if no path found

Time: O(n * m!) - For each word, we explore all permutations of remaining words
Space: O(m) - Maximum recursion depth and path storage

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Backtracking Exploration / Pattern Matching
ladders.desc: The recursive function that explores all possible transformation paths from a given word.
ladders.explanation:
This recursive backtracking function is the heart of the brute force approach. The intuition is that to find all possible paths to the destination, we need to systematically explore every valid branching possibility from each word, similar to exploring all paths in a maze. The function takes the current word, current path, and set of visited words in this path. It adds the current word to the path, then if it's the destination, it records the path if it's the shortest found so far. Otherwise, it finds all words in the list that differ by exactly one character from the current word and haven't been visited in this path, then recursively explores each one. After exploring all possibilities from the current word, it backtracks by removing the word from the path and visited set. This ensures we explore all permutations of words. Time complexity for this subroutine is O(n * m) per call where n is word length and m is word list size, but the overall exploration creates factorial complexity. Space complexity is O(m) for recursion depth. This connects to L0 by being the main recursive engine that drives the complete solution. Common mistakes include forgetting to backtrack properly (leading to incorrect paths), not copying the visited set correctly (causing unintended sharing between recursive calls), and inefficient neighbor finding. The real code pattern is a classic backtracking template with base case, exploration, and cleanup.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Function backtrack(current_word, current_path, visited_in_path, shortest_path_ref):
   a. Add current_word to current_path and visited_in_path
   b. If current_word == end_word:
      i. If current_path shorter than shortest_path_ref[0], update it
   c. Else:
      i. For each word in word_list:
         - If one_char_diff(current_word, word) AND word not in visited_in_path:
            * backtrack(word, current_path, visited_in_path, shortest_path_ref)
   d. Remove current_word from current_path and visited_in_path

Time: O(n * m) per call (but called many times)
Space: O(m) for recursion stack

examples.trace:
Step 1: Add 'hot' to path (already there) and visited
Step 2: 'hot' != 'cog' (end word), so explore neighbors
Step 3: Check 'dot': differs from 'hot' by 1 char? 'h'→'d', yes. Not in visited? Yes.
Step 4: Recursively call with 'dot', path=['hit','hot','dot'], visited={'hit','hot','dot'}
Step 5: From that call, will explore 'dog', etc.
Step 6: Return from 'dot' recursion, remove 'dot' from path and visited
Step 7: Check 'lot': differs by 1 char? 'h'→'l', yes. Not visited? Yes.
Step 8: Recursively call with 'lot', path=['hit','hot','lot'], visited
... (simplified)

Step 1: Add 'cog' to path (already there) and visited
Step 2: 'cog' == 'cog' (end word)! Base case reached.
Step 3: Current path length = 5
Step 4: If no shorter path recorded yet, store this path as shortest
Step 5: Remove 'cog' from path and visited (backtrack)
Step 6: Return to caller ('dog')

ladders.title: Core Logic — One-Character Difference / BFS Traversal
ladders.desc: Determining if two words differ by exactly one character, enabling valid transformations.
ladders.explanation:
This core logic determines whether a transformation between two words is valid in the word ladder problem. The intuition is that in a word ladder, you can only change one letter at a time, so we need to efficiently check if two words differ by exactly one character position. This is fundamental because it defines which words are neighbors in our search graph. The algorithm compares two words character by character, counting differences. If the words have different lengths, they cannot differ by one character (though typically all words have same length in this problem). We iterate through each position, incrementing a difference counter when characters don't match. If the counter exceeds 1, we can early return false. At the end, we return true if exactly one difference was found. Time complexity is O(n) where n is word length. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L1 by being the condition that determines which words to explore recursively from the current word. Common mistakes include not handling words of different lengths correctly, counting differences incorrectly (off-by-one errors), and not optimizing with early termination. The real code pattern is a simple loop with a difference counter.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. If len(word1) != len(word2): return false
2. Initialize diff_count = 0
3. For i from 0 to len(word1)-1:
   a. If word1[i] != word2[i]:
      i. diff_count += 1
      ii. If diff_count > 1: return false
4. Return diff_count == 1

Time: O(n) where n is word length
Space: O(1)

examples.trace:
Step 1: Both length 3, continue
Step 2: Compare position 0: 'h' == 'h', same
Step 3: Compare position 1: 'i' != 'o', diff_count=1
Step 4: Compare position 2: 't' == 't', same
Step 5: diff_count=1, return true

Step 1: Both length 3, continue
Step 2: Compare position 0: 'h' != 'c', diff_count=1
Step 3: Compare position 1: 'i' != 'o', diff_count=2
Step 4: diff_count>1, return false early

ladders.title: Building Block — Neighbor Generation / Hash Set Lookup
ladders.desc: Identifying all words in a list that differ by one character from a given word.
ladders.explanation:
This building block finds all possible next words from a current word in the transformation sequence. The intuition is that to explore paths forward, we need to know which words can be reached from the current word with a single character change. This creates the branching factor in our search tree. The algorithm iterates through all words in the word list, applying the one-character difference check to each, and collects those that pass. We also exclude words that have already been visited in the current path to avoid cycles. Time complexity is O(n * m) where n is word length and m is list size, as we check each word. Space complexity is O(m) in worst case for storing all neighbors. This connects to L2 by using the one-character difference check repeatedly to filter the word list. Common mistakes include not filtering visited words (causing infinite recursion), inefficient repeated checking, and not handling empty lists correctly. The real code pattern is a list comprehension or filter with the difference check.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize neighbors = empty list
2. For each word in word_list:
   a. If word != current_word AND one_char_diff(current_word, word) AND word not in visited_set:
      i. Add word to neighbors
3. Return neighbors

Time: O(n * m) where n=word length, m=list size
Space: O(m) for neighbors list

examples.trace:
Step 1: Check 'dot': != 'hot', diff by 1? 'h'→'d', yes, not in visited? yes → add
Step 2: Check 'lot': != 'hot', diff by 1? 'h'→'l', yes, not visited? yes → add
Step 3: Check 'dog': != 'hot', diff by 1? 'h'→'d' and 't'→'g' = 2 changes, no
Step 4: Check 'hit': != 'hot', diff by 1? 'o'→'i', yes, but 'hit' in visited → skip
Step 5: Check 'cot': != 'hot', diff by 1? 'h'→'c', yes, not visited? yes → add
Step 6: Return ['dot','lot','cot']

Step 1: Check 'b': != 'a', diff by 1? yes (single char), not visited → add
Step 2: Check 'c': != 'a', diff by 1? yes, not visited → add
Step 3: Check 'd': != 'a', diff by 1? yes, not visited → add
Step 4: Return ['b','c','d']

ladders.title: Basic Operation — String Manipulation / Character Comparison
ladders.desc: Comparing two strings character by character to count differences.
ladders.explanation:
This basic operation involves iterating through two strings and comparing each corresponding character. The intuition is that strings are sequences of characters, and to understand their relationship (like how many positions differ), we need to examine each position individually. This is a fundamental programming operation used in many string algorithms. The algorithm simply loops through indices from 0 to length-1 (assuming equal lengths), accessing characters at each position and comparing them. We can count matches, differences, or perform other per-character operations. Time complexity is O(n) where n is string length. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L3 by being the mechanism used within the one-character difference check - we build upon simple character comparison to implement the more complex neighbor validation. Common mistakes include index out of bounds errors (when strings have different lengths), confusing character comparison with string comparison, and inefficient repeated character access. The real code pattern is a for loop with string indexing.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. If len(str1) != len(str2): handle appropriately (return false or error)
2. Initialize counter = 0
3. For i from 0 to len(str1)-1:
   a. char1 = str1[i]
   b. char2 = str2[i]
   c. Compare char1 and char2 (e.g., increment counter if different)
4. Return result based on counter

Time: O(n)
Space: O(1)

examples.trace:
Step 1: Both length 3
Step 2: i=0: 'c' != 'b', difference found
Step 3: i=1: 'a' == 'a', same
Step 4: i=2: 't' == 't', same
Step 5: Total differences: 1

Step 1: Both length 5
Step 2: i=0: 'h' == 'h'
Step 3: i=1: 'e' == 'e'
Step 4: i=2: 'l' == 'l'
Step 5: i=3: 'l' == 'l'
Step 6: i=4: 'o' == 'o'
Step 7: Total differences: 0

ladders.desc: Basic understanding of modeling word transformations as graphs.
ladders.explanation: In Word Ladder, each word is a node. An edge exists between two words if they differ by exactly one character. BFS finds the shortest transformation sequence (shortest path).

approaches.name: BFS with Pattern Matching
approaches.summary: Perform BFS without pre-building the graph by generating all possible one-character variations of the current word and checking if they exist in the word list using a hash set. This optimizes space by avoiding adjacency list storage.

ladders.title: Full Problem — BFS with Pattern Matching
ladders.desc: Complete solution using BFS with pattern matching to find shortest transformation sequence between words.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is to treat the word transformation as a graph traversal problem where each word is a node, and edges exist between words that differ by exactly one character. Instead of pre-building the entire graph (which would be O(n²) for n words), we use BFS while dynamically generating neighbor candidates. For each current word in BFS, we generate all possible one-character variations by replacing each position with all 26 letters, then check if these variations exist in our word set. This avoids storing adjacency lists, saving significant space. The BFS ensures we find the shortest path since it explores level by level. We use a visited set to avoid cycles and a queue for BFS traversal. Time complexity is O(n * m) where n is number of words and m is word length (for generating variations), and space is O(n) for storing the word set and visited information. This connects to L1 by using the pattern matching subroutine to efficiently find neighbors. Common mistakes include not handling the case where end word isn't in word list, forgetting to remove visited words from the word set to prevent revisiting, and not using proper BFS termination. Real code pattern: Initialize word set, queue with (beginWord, 1), visited set. While queue not empty: pop word, level. If word equals endWord, return level. For each position in word: for each letter a-z: create new word by replacing character. If new word in word set: add to queue, remove from word set. Return 0 if no path found.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize wordSet = {'hot','dot','dog','lot','log','cog'}, queue = [('hit',1)], visited = {}. Step 2: Pop ('hit',1). Not 'cog'. Generate variations: For position 0: 'ait','bit',...,'hot' (found in set). Add ('hot',2) to queue, remove 'hot' from set. Others like 'pit' not in set. Position 1: 'hat','hbt',...,'hzt' none in set. Position 2: 'hia','hib',...,'hiz' none in set. Step 3: Pop ('hot',2). Generate variations: Position 0: 'aot','bot',...,'lot' (found). Add ('lot',3), remove 'lot'. 'dot' (found). Add ('dot',3), remove 'dot'. Others 
... (simplified)

Step 1: wordSet = {'hot','dog'}, queue = [('hot',1)]. Step 2: Pop ('hot',1). Not 'dog'. Generate variations: Position 0: 'aot','bot',...,'zot' none in set (except 'hot' itself). Position 1: 'hat','hbt',...,'hzt' none. Position 2: 'hoa','hob',...,'hoz' none. No new words added to queue. Step 3: Queue empty, return 0 (no transformation sequence).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Backtracking Exploration / Pattern Matching
ladders.desc: Generate all one-character variations of a word and check against a word set.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that instead of comparing the current word with every other word in the list (O(n) per word), we can efficiently find neighbors by generating all possible words that differ by one character. For a word of length m, there are m * 26 possible variations (since each of m positions can be replaced with 26 letters). By checking these against a hash set of valid words (O(1) average lookup), we find neighbors in O(m * 26) time, which is much faster than O(n) when n is large. This subroutine is the core optimization in the BFS approach, avoiding pre-computation of the graph. Step-by-step: For a given word, iterate through each position i from 0 to m-1. For each position, iterate through all 26 lowercase letters. Create a new word by replacing the character at position i with the current letter. If this new word exists in the word set, it's a valid neighbor. We typically remove found words from the set to mark them as visited. Time complexity is O(m * 26) = O(m) since 26 is constant, and space is O(1) extra beyond the word set. This feeds into L0 by being called for each word during BFS traversal. Common mistakes include not handling uppercase letters (assume lowercase), generating variations that include the original word (which should be skipped or handled via visited set), and inefficient string manipulation (use list conversion for mutation). Real code pattern: neighbors = []. For i in range(len(word)): for c in 'abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz': if c != word[i]: new_word = word[:i] + c + word[i+1:]. If new_word in word_set: neighbors.append(new_word). Return neighbors.

examples.trace: Step 1: Word length 3. Position 0: Generate 'aot','bot',...,'zot'. 'dot' found in set, add to neighbors. 'lot' found, add. Others not. Position 1: Generate 'hat','hbt',...,'hzt'. None in set. Position 2: Generate 'hoa','hob',...,'hoz'. None in set. Final neighbors: ['dot','lot'].

Step 1: Position 0: 'aat','bat' (found), 'cat' (original, skip), 'dat',...,'zat'. 'bat' added. Position 1: 'cbt','cct','cdt',...,'czt'. None in set. Position 2: 'caa','cab',...,'caz'. None in set. Final neighbors: ['bat','mat'] from position 0 variations.

ladders.title: Core Logic — One-Character Difference / BFS Traversal
ladders.desc: Use BFS to find shortest transformation sequence in an implicit graph.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind using BFS is that it explores all nodes level by level from the start node, guaranteeing that the first time we reach the target node, we've found the shortest path in terms of number of edges (transformations). In the word ladder problem, each transformation (changing one character) is an edge, and BFS ensures we minimize the number of transformations. We treat the word list as an implicit graph where nodes are words, and edges exist between words that differ by one character. BFS uses a queue to process nodes in FIFO order, a visited set to avoid cycles, and tracks the current level or distance. Step-by-step: Initialize a queue with the start word and level 1. While queue is not empty, dequeue a word and its level. If it's the target word, return the level. Otherwise, generate its neighbors (using pattern matching from L1), add unvisited neighbors to the queue with level+1, and mark them as visited. If queue empties without finding the target, return 0 or an indicator of no path. Time complexity depends on neighbor generation but in worst case O(n * m) for n words and m length, and space is O(n) for queue and visited storage. This is the central technique in L0, combined with pattern matching. Common mistakes include using DFS instead (which doesn't guarantee shortest path), not marking nodes as visited before enqueuing (causing duplicates), and incorrect level tracking. Real code pattern: queue = deque([(start, 1)]). visited = set([start]). While queue: word, level = queue.popleft(). If word == target: return level. For neighbor in get_neighbors(word): if neighbor not in visited: queue.append((neighbor, level+1)); visited.add(neighbor). Return 0.

examples.input: start = 'hot', target = 'dog', neighbors_func returns neighbors, word list implicit
examples.trace: Step 1: queue = [('hot',1)], visited = {'hot'}. Step 2: Dequeue ('hot',1). Not 'dog'. Get neighbors: ['dot','lot']. Add ('dot',2) and ('lot',2) to queue, visited = {'hot','dot','lot'}. Step 3: Dequeue ('dot',2). Not 'dog'. Neighbors: ['dog'] (assuming 'dog' in list). Add ('dog',3) to queue, visited add 'dog'. Step 4: Dequeue ('lot',2). Not 'dog'. Neighbors: [] (assuming no new). Step 5: Dequeue ('dog',3). Equals target, return 3.

Step 1: queue = [('cat',1)], visited = {'cat'}. Step 2: Dequeue ('cat',1). Not 'bat'. Neighbors: ['bat'] (if 'bat' in list). Add ('bat',2) to queue, visited add 'bat'. Step 3: Dequeue ('bat',2). Equals target, return 2.

testCases.input: start = 'A', target = 'D', neighbors: A->B, B->C, C->D
start = 'X', target = 'Y', neighbors: X->Z, Z->W, no path to Y
start = 'S', target = 'T', neighbors: S->U, U->V, V->T
start = 'G', target = 'H', neighbors: G->I, I->J, J->H
start = 'Q', target = 'R', neighbors: Q->S, S->T, T->R
start = 'U', target = 'V', neighbors: U->W, W->X, X->V

ladders.title: Building Block — Neighbor Generation / Hash Set Lookup
ladders.desc: Use a hash set to store words for efficient existence checks.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that we need to quickly check if a generated word variation exists in the word list. A hash set provides average O(1) time for insertion, deletion, and lookup, which is crucial for the pattern matching subroutine. By converting the word list to a set, we avoid linear scans that would take O(n) time per check, making the overall algorithm O(n * m) instead of O(n² * m). This building block is essential in L1 and L0 to enable efficient neighbor discovery. Step-by-step: Given a list of words, create a set by adding each word. During BFS, when we generate a new word variation, we check if it's in the set using the 'in' operator. If it is, we can add it to the queue and optionally remove it from the set to prevent revisiting (which also serves as a visited marker). Time complexity for set operations is O(1) on average, and space is O(n) to store all words. This connects to L2 by providing the data structure for BFS to efficiently find next nodes. Common mistakes include not converting the list to a set (leading to O(n) lookups), using a list for lookups, or not handling duplicate words in the input list (sets automatically handle duplicates). Real code pattern: word_set = set(wordList). Then, to check: if candidate_word in word_set: do something. To remove: word_set.remove(candidate_word).

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize empty set. Step 2: Add 'hot' -> set {'hot'}. Step 3: Add 'dot' -> {'hot','dot'}. Step 4: Add 'dog' -> {'hot','dot','dog'}. Step 5: Add 'lot' -> {'hot','dot','dog','lot'}. Step 6: Add 'log' -> {'hot','dot','dog','lot','log'}. Step 7: Add 'cog' -> {'hot','dot','dog','lot','log','cog'}. Final set has 6 elements.

Step 1: Empty set. Step 2: Add 'cat' -> {'cat'}. Step 3: Add 'bat' -> {'cat','bat'}. Step 4: Add 'mat' -> {'cat','bat','mat'}. Step 5: Add 'cat' again, set ignores duplicate, remains {'cat','bat','mat'}. Step 6: Add 'bat' again, ignored. Final set has 3 unique elements.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — String Manipulation / Character Comparison
ladders.desc: Generate a new string by replacing one character at a specific position.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that to find words that differ by one character, we need to create new strings by changing exactly one character in the original word. This basic operation involves string indexing and concatenation. Since strings are immutable in many languages, we typically convert the string to a list of characters, modify the desired index, and then join back into a string. This operation is performed for each position and each possible letter in the pattern matching subroutine. Step-by-step: Given a word string and a position pos (0-indexed) and a new character c, we can create the new word by taking the substring before pos, adding c, and then the substring after pos. Alternatively, convert to list: char_list = list(word); char_list[pos] = c; new_word = ''.join(char_list). Time complexity is O(m) for creating the new string where m is word length, and space is O(m) for the new string. This feeds into L3 and L1 as part of generating neighbors. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in indexing, not handling empty strings, or inefficient string concatenation in loops. Real code pattern: new_word = word[:pos] + c + word[pos+1:]. Or using list: chars = list(word); chars[pos] = c; new_word = ''.join(chars).

examples.trace: Step 1: word = 'hot', pos = 0, c = 'd'. Step 2: Substring before pos: '' (since pos 0). Step 3: Add c: 'd'. Step 4: Substring after pos: 'ot' (from index 1 to end). Step 5: Concatenate: '' + 'd' + 'ot' = 'dot'.

Step 1: word = 'test', pos = 2, c = 'a'. Step 2: Substring before pos: 'te' (indices 0-1). Step 3: Add c: 'a'. Step 4: Substring after pos: 't' (index 3). Step 5: Concatenate: 'te' + 'a' + 't' = 'teat'.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graphs & Word Transformations
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of modeling word transformations as graphs.
ladders.explanation: In Word Ladder, each word is a node. An edge exists between two words if they differ by exactly one character. BFS finds the shortest transformation sequence (shortest path).

examples.output: Graph: hot connected to dot; dot connected to hot and dog; dog connected to dot
examples.trace: Step 1: Nodes: 'hot', 'dot', 'dog'. Step 2: Check 'hot' and 'dot': differ at position 0 (h vs d), so edge between hot and dot. Step 3: Check 'hot' and 'dog': differ at multiple positions, no edge. Step 4: Check 'dot' and 'dog': differ at position 2 (t vs g), so edge between dot and dog. Final graph edges: hot-dot, dot-dog.

examples.output: Graph: cat connected to bat and mat; bat connected to cat; mat connected to cat
examples.trace: Step 1: Nodes: 'cat', 'bat', 'mat'. Step 2: Check 'cat' and 'bat': differ at position 0 (c vs b), edge. Step 3: Check 'cat' and 'mat': differ at position 0 (c vs m), edge. Step 4: Check 'bat' and 'mat': differ at position 0 (b vs m), edge. All pairs connected since all differ by one character in first position.

testCases.input: words = ['A','B'] where A and B differ by one character
words = ['A','B','C'] where A-B differ, B-C differ, A-C not differ
words = ['X','Y','Z'] all differ by one character from each other
testCases.expected: Graph: X-Y edge, X-Z edge, Y-Z edge

testCases.input: words = ['P','Q'] where P and Q are identical
words = ['M','N'] where M and N differ by more than one character
words = ['at','it','et'] all differ by one character from each other
testCases.expected: Graph: at-it edge, at-et edge, it-et edge
